text: hello and welcome respected chairpersons and delegates i will be presenting ventral mesh rectopexy for recurrent rectal prolapse this is a sixty seven year old lady who complains of mass descending per rectum for six months which could be reduced manually she doesn't give history of constipation though she is suffering from depression for last ten years she also underwent rectal prolapse surgery twice once in the form of thiers procedure followed by and delorme's procedure one year back On examination, per abdomen is soft. Per rectally, the previously polypropylene sutures of Delorme's repair could be palpated. Patient is in tender and back position with the ports placed as shown. A polyamide suture on straight needle is used to elevate the uterus, which is help us in proper visualization of the deep cul-de-sac. The rectum is elevated.

The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory and makes an incision over the peritoneum at this location, entering the retrorectal space. The peritoneum is divided, allowing entry into the presacral plane, which is visually avascular. A monopolar device is used for hemostatic dissection. An inverted J-shaped incision is marked and tattooed on the right side of the peritoneum.

The surgeon begins anterior dissection by lifting and dividing the rectovaginal septum using two instruments and monopolar energy. The rectal anterior surface is exposed as the fat is removed with the septum. Dissection continues up to the perineal body, with the assistant confirming the positions of the vagina and rectum by palpation. Ligaments and attachments are divided to fully mobilize the prolapsed rectum.

The surgeon preserves the lateral ligament on the right side during posterior dissection, which continues up to the pelvic floor. The dissection halts upon reaching the pelvic floor, and the length of mesh required is measured using an umbilical cotton tape.

Ethibond IIO sutures are used to fix the mesh to the anterior surface of the rectum, followed by fixation to the sacral promontory. Two sutures are placed to secure the mesh and prevent rectal prolapse. The mesh is further fixed along the mesentery of the rectum, and fibrin glue is applied to the anterior wall of the rectum. The peritoneum is closed with a V-lock running suture to extraperitonealize the mesh, concluding the procedure.
